  69. Welsh PW, Reed CE, Conrad E. Timing of once-a-day theophylline dose to match peak blood level with diurnal variation in severity of asthma. Am J Med 1986; 80:1098.
  70. Kraft M, Torvik JA, Trudeau JB, et al. Theophylline: potential antiinflammatory effects in nocturnal asthma. J Allergy Clin Immunol 1996; 97:1242.
  71. Selby C, Engleman HM, Fitzpatrick MF, et al. Inhaled salmeterol or oral theophylline in nocturnal asthma? Am J Respir Crit Care Med 1997; 155:104.
  72. Spector SL, Smith LJ, Glass M. Effects of 6 weeks of therapy with oral doses of ICI 204,219, a leukotriene D4 receptor antagonist, in subjects with bronchial asthma. ACCOLATE Asthma Trialists Group. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 1994; 150:618.
  73. Malmstrom K, Rodriguez-Gomez G, Guerra J, et al. Oral montelukast, inhaled beclomethasone, and placebo for chronic asthma. A randomized, controlled trial. Montelukast/Beclomethasone Study Group. Ann Intern Med 1999; 130:487.
  74. Esmailpour N, Högger P, Rabe KF, et al. Distribution of inhaled fluticasone propionate between human lung tissue and serum in vivo. Eur Respir J 1997; 10:1496.

Topic 524 Version 8.0

---

# Virus-induced wheezing and asthma: An overview

Virus-induced wheezing and asthma: An overview

Author:
    Sujani Kakumanu, MD
Section Editors:
    Gregory Redding, MD
    Robert A Wood, MD
Deputy Editor:
    Elizabeth TePas, MD, MS

Contributor Disclosures

All topics are updated as new evidence becomes available and our peer review process is complete.

Literature review current through: Feb 2018. | This topic last updated: Sep 02, 2016.

INTRODUCTION — Viral respiratory infections are the most common causes of wheezing in infants and young children and are common triggers of asthma exacerbations in adult and pediatric patients with preexisting asthma [1-4]. In addition, wheezing with viral illnesses, particularly with respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) and human rhinovirus (HRV), in infants and young children is associated with an increased risk of developing childhood asthma [5-10].

The mechanisms by which viral respiratory infections trigger wheezing and asthma exacerbations are reviewed here. The influence of viral infection on both the development and perpetuation of asthma is also discussed. Other issues relating to the pathogenesis of asthma are reviewed elsewhere. Treatment of virus-induced wheezing and asthma is also discussed separately. (See "Pathogenesis of asthma" and "Risk factors for asthma" and "Natural history of asthma" and "Treatment of recurrent virus-induced wheezing in young children".)

PATHOGENESIS — Viral respiratory infections interact with host factors to promote recurrent virus-induced wheezing and the development of asthma (figure 1) [11]. Respiratory symptoms with viral infections most likely result from virus-induced damage of the airway epithelium, followed by airway inflammation in a predisposed individual. Immune responses triggered by viral respiratory tract infections include enhanced allergic inflammation, increased granulocyte recruitment, promotion of cytokine production, and upregulation of the parasympathetic response (table 1). These proinflammatory responses further influence the host responses to other microbial infections, allergens, stress, and pollutants.

●**Epithelial cells** – Respiratory epithelial cells are the initial site of viral inoculation and serve as the primary site of viral replication. Respiratory viruses, such as influenza and respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), cause cytopathic damage to airway epithelium, and these changes can affect the function of the epithelium and airway smooth muscle. In addition, disturbing the integrity of the epithelial layer may enhance mucosal permeability and increase exposure of inflammatory and antigen-presenting cells to allergens, other infections, and irritants.

Approximately 90 percent of rhinovirus human rhinovirus-A (HRV-A) and human rhinovirus-B (HRV-B) serotypes bind to respiratory epithelial cells via the intercellular adhesion molecule 1 (ICAM-1, also called CD54 [cluster of differentiation 54]), while a minority, belonging to the HRV-A group, bind to the very low density lipoprotein (VLDL) receptor [12,13]. CDHR3, a human cadherin-related family member 3 protein, mediates human rhinovirus-C (HRV-C) entry into host cells [14]. The receptor for HRV-C strains has not yet been identified.

Rhinoviruses, in contrast to other respiratory viruses, do not damage the epithelium. However, rhinovirus and other respiratory viruses can activate epithelial cells to upregulate the expression of ICAM-1 receptors, enhancing replication of HRV. They also induce epithelial cells to secrete a wide variety of chemokines and cytokines, including IL-6, IL-8 (also called CXCL8 [chemokine, C-X-C motif, ligand 8]), IL-11, and RANTES (regulated upon activation, normally T expressed, and presumably secreted; also called CCL5 [chemokine, C-C motif, ligand 5]), a chemoattractant for eosinophils, monocytes, and T cells [15-21]. In addition, viral infections may also induce the release of epithelial mediators such as thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), IL-25, and IL-33, which promote T helper cell type 2 (Th2) inflammation, particularly in patients with atopy and asthma.

Many of these mediators have been identified in experimental or natural infections and can affect cell recruitment, cell activation, and the overall inflammatory response. Furthermore, in vitro work showing upregulation of major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class I and costimulatory molecules, B-lymphocyte activation antigens B7-1 (CD80) and B7-2 (CD86), in pulmonary epithelial cells after rhinovirus infection suggests that epithelial cells may play a role in antigen presentation [22].

●**Dendritic cells** – Several investigations have implicated airway mucosa dendritic cells as potent antigen presenting cells and mediators of the synergistic inflammation induced by the presence of atopy and viral infections [23-25]. Specifically, upregulation and crosslinking of the FcEpsilon receptor on lung dendritic cells may promote Th2 inflammation and modulate type I interferon antiviral responses [26].

●**Airway inflammation** – Respiratory viral infection and replication activate innate and adaptive antiviral immune responses, resulting in proinflammatory cytokine and chemokine production and the recruitment of inflammatory cells to the airway. Rhinovirus has shown few cytopathic effects on airway epithelium or other tissues in contrast to other respiratory viruses, such as RSV and influenza [27-29]. These observations raise the possibility that rhinovirus can induce and promote airway inflammation by its interaction with airway cells and the subsequent stimulation of cytokine production.

Rhinovirus infection was experimentally induced in a number of studies, and the subsequent response to antigen challenge was evaluated [30-35]. Experimental infections with rhinovirus lead to increased airway responsiveness, enhanced inflammatory responses, a higher likelihood for the development of the late-allergic reaction, and eosinophil recruitment to the airway following antigen challenge. The histologic changes seen with experimental infection are also seen in biopsies from individuals with naturally occurring rhinovirus colds. Collectively, these studies support the view that rhinovirus promotes lower airway inflammation to allergen and can thereby enhance existing allergic inflammation. The following studies are illustrative:

•Two studies found increased airway responsiveness to histamine and augmentation of both the immediate- and late-phase responses to inhaled allergen following experimental rhinovirus infection in patients with allergic rhinitis (figure 2) [30,31]. In one of these reports, rhinovirus infection enhanced the allergic inflammatory reaction in the lower airway, as demonstrated by increased recruitment of eosinophils to the airway following segmental antigen challenge [30].

•Increased submucosal lymphocytes and epithelial eosinophils are demonstrated on biopsy of the lower airway during rhinovirus colds that are associated with increased airway responsiveness to histamine [33]. The increase in mucosal eosinophils persists well into the convalescent period in subjects with asthma. In another study, nasal lavage eosinophil counts prior to experimental rhinovirus infection were inversely correlated with the severity and duration of subsequent cold symptoms [32].

•Studies have also shown that rhinovirus replicates equally well in the upper and lower airways [34]. In these studies, bronchial epithelial cells from patients with asthma show markedly increased rhinovirus replication compared with those without asthma. In addition, rhinovirus can enter the lower airway and stimulate monocytes/macrophages and lymphocytes [34]. This interaction can lead to the recruitment of inflammatory cells to the airway and generation of proinflammatory and antiviral mediators.

●**Granulocytes** – Neutrophils are the principal cells recruited to the airways during the acute phase of a viral infection and are also recruited to the lower airways during experimental rhinovirus infection [36]. During acute viral illness, HRV-infected adults with asthma have increased recruitment of neutrophils compared with HRV-infected adults without asthma [37]. This response is, in part, regulated by IL-8 (CXCL8) and leukotriene B4 (LTB4) [15,38-42]. There is evidence that the recruited cells are activated. Levels of airway granulocyte enzymes (eg, myeloperoxidase) increase in parallel with the recruitment of neutrophils. The mechanism by which these cells are activated during the viral illness is not well understood.

The interrelationship between eosinophils and viral infections is less well defined [43-46]. There is little evidence from in vitro studies that rhinoviruses activate eosinophils, in contrast to in vivo observations. Eosinophils are increased in the bronchial epithelium in biopsies of both patients with asthma and controls during acute rhinovirus infection [33]. Increased levels of leukotriene C4 (LTC4) are seen in nasal secretions of children with wheezing during RSV infections [46]. This finding suggests that eosinophil recruitment to the airway and activation events may be directly related to RSV actions.

●**Macrophages and monocytes** – Airway macrophages are the predominant airway cell and are probably involved in host responses to respiratory viruses. Nasal secretions obtained during upper respiratory tract infections contain IL-1, tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-alpha), and IL-8 [39,40,47,48]. A similar cytokine pattern is seen when macrophages are incubated with respiratory viruses in vitro [15,49-51].

These cytokines are important in the development of acute symptoms, such as fever and myalgia. In addition, these mediators can stimulate epithelial cells to express adhesion molecules, such as ICAM-1 [50]. This upregulation of adhesion molecule expression can have a dual effect of increasing both receptor binding sites for rhinovirus and ligands for inflammatory cells. The end result is an increase in the inflammatory response.

●**Lymphocytes** – Respiratory viruses can provoke specific and nonspecific responses in T cells [52]. Natural killer cells, CD4+ T cells, and CD8+ T cells all participate in antiviral immune responses. As an example, CD8+ cells are a major source of TNF-alpha, which is important for antiviral effects, but can also stimulate epithelial cells to express ICAM-1.

Virus-specific T-cell responses are not detected in the airway or blood until 7 to 10 days following inoculation. The timing of this response raises questions about the role of virus-specific T cells in acute wheezing episodes. In contrast, lung T cells are probably activated in an antigen or virus-independent manner through "bystander" effects. As an example, incubation of peripheral blood monocytes with rhinovirus leads to increased expression of the early activation marker, CD69, on approximately 50 percent of T cells. Associated with this response is an increase in messenger ribonucleic acid (mRNA) for interferon-gamma (IFN-gamma) [53].

In mice, antigen-independent activation of T cells occurs during systemic viral infection with lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus, a viral surrogate that is used instead of RSV or HRV. Other examples exist and suggest that respiratory viruses can cause nonspecific T-cell recruitment and activation early in the course of a viral infection. The timing of this response suggests that it could serve to enhance existing airway inflammation and consequently exacerbate underlying asthma [54].

●**Cytokine release** – Viruses most likely enhance allergic inflammation and promote asthma exacerbations through the release of proinflammatory cytokines. A number of investigators have found increased concentrations of cytokines, including IL-1, IL-2, IFN-alpha, and IFN-gamma, in nasal aspirates and from activated peripheral blood cells at the time of a cold [47,55-58]. These observations raise the possibility that rhinovirus either stimulates cytokine production, thereby changing the microenvironment of the lower airway, or enhances the capacity of cells to generate cytokines in response to other stimuli. The end result of this effect on cytokine production is an increased potential for allergic inflammation during an encounter with other factors, such as allergens.

Virus-induced wheezing is also linked to deficiencies in antiviral innate immunity, specifically IFN production in response to viral infection. The interferons may play several roles in asthma.

Type I interferons (IFN-alpha, IFN-beta) are important in antiviral immunity, and patients with asthma have decreased virus-induced IFN-beta compared with those without asthma [59]. Furthermore, an inverse correlation was found between peak cold symptoms and the IFN-gamma to IL-5 mRNA ratio in atopic subjects with experimental rhinovirus infection [60,61]. The mechanisms for decreased IFN-production are not known, but may be related to deficient expression of Toll-like receptors, such as TLR3 and TLR7/8, or in molecules involved in signaling cascades.

Abnormalities in IFN-lambda production are described in patients with asthma [59]. In vitro research has shown that baseline IFN-lambda1-3 production is deficient in bronchial epithelial cells and alveolar macrophages in patients with asthma [62]. A prospective study in school-aged children with asthma reported increased IFN-lambda1 production in acutely HRV-infected wheezing children compared with HRV-infected nonwheezers, suggesting that IFN-lambda1 production may modulate HRV-induced asthma exacerbations [63]. Further research is needed to determine the definitive role of IFN-lambda in virus-induced wheezing.

TSLP is released in HRV- and RSV-infected human bronchial epithelial cells, resulting in increased dendritic and mast cell expression and a potent Th2 inflammatory cascade. These effects can potentially lead to airway hyperreactivity in response to viral infection [64,65].

●**Parasympathetic hyperresponsiveness** – In animal studies, other mechanisms have been identified to explain changes in airway function during viral respiratory infections. Two studies in guinea pigs and rats found that viral respiratory infections impair inhibitory subtype 2 muscarinic receptor (M2) function, producing parasympathetic hyperresponsiveness that leads to bronchoconstriction (figure 3) [66,67]. This effect may persist for weeks after resolution of the acute infection [67]. Glucocorticoid administration reverses the virus-associated changes in airway function [67].

●**Increased immunoglobulin E (IgE)** – One study demonstrated increases in IgE levels in subjects with allergic rhinitis, but not in subjects with nonallergic rhinitis after experimental rhinovirus infection [68]. Other investigators have shown that RSV and parainfluenza virus can stimulate total IgE and virus-specific IgE in children [69-71]. The development of virus-specific IgE levels correlates with the probability of recurrent episodes of wheeze.

WHEEZING DUE TO VIRAL INFECTION — There is increasing evidence that immunoglobulin E (IgE) may mediate viral responses to infection [24,72]. Studies have shown that viral infections trigger up to 85 percent of asthma exacerbations in school-aged children and up to 50 percent of exacerbations in adults [73,74]. The clinical implications and differential diagnosis of virus-induced wheezing vary with the age of the patient.

Children <2 years of age — A first episode of wheezing in association with a viral respiratory infection leads to an outpatient visit for approximately 15 percent of all infants in the United States, and 3 percent are hospitalized with this presentation [75,76]. Bronchiolitis, or inflammation of the bronchioles, is a clinical syndrome characterized by wheezing, tachypnea, and hyperinflation of the lungs [77]. It is of primary importance in infants and young children less than two years of age. (See "Bronchiolitis in infants and children: Clinical features and diagnosis", section on 'Clinical features'.)

●Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is the most common cause of bronchiolitis and wheezing in this age group. One study examined the causative factors for acute wheezing in infants and children presenting to the emergency department [78]. RSV was the most common infectious agent found in children less than two years of age, and the affected children were nonallergic. (See "Respiratory syncytial virus infection: Clinical features and diagnosis".)

●Rhinovirus is also a common cause of wheezing and bronchiolitis in infancy, although its prevalence in virus-induced wheezing is more significant in adults and children older than two years of age [5,9,79-81]. The human rhinovirus-C (HRV-C) serotypes, in addition to human rhinovirus-A (HRV-A) and human rhinovirus-B (HRV-B), are associated with virus-induced wheezing and asthma exacerbations in children [82-84]. (See "The common cold in children: Clinical features and diagnosis", section on 'Virology' and "Epidemiology, clinical manifestations, and pathogenesis of rhinovirus infections".)

●Metapneumovirus, another member of the _Paramyxoviridae_ family, can cause upper and lower respiratory tract infection and wheezing in young children [85,86]. (See "Human metapneumovirus infections".)

●Coronaviruses, bocaviruses, and influenza can also provoke wheezing [87-90]. (See "Coronaviruses" and "Seasonal influenza in children: Clinical features and diagnosis", section on 'Clinical features'.)

Risk factors for wheezing — A number of epidemiologic studies have investigated risk factors for wheezing with respiratory infections and the effect of these illnesses on the eventual development of asthma [91-95]. (See "Risk factors for asthma", section on 'Respiratory infections' and 'Development of asthma' below.)

●Exposure to cigarette smoke is a major predictor of wheezing in children less than two years of age [96].

●Many children who wheeze with RSV infection appear to have reduced lung function, which may predispose them to developing wheezing with respiratory infections [95,97,98]. A prospective analysis of 1246 children, for example, assessed the relationship between initial lung function and the development of a wheezing illness during the first year of life [98]. Infants with low total respiratory conductance had an almost fourfold higher risk of wheezing with viral respiratory infections. These data indicate that lung size is an important factor in the manifestation of wheezing with an RSV infection. Boys generally have smaller lungs than girls, which may explain in part why boys are nearly 10 times more likely than girls to wheeze after respiratory infections [98]. Furthermore, infants with lower lung function are at increased risk of airflow obstruction as young adults [99].

●Risk factors associated with recurrent wheezing in children aged 12 to 15 months in a cross-sectional study included parental history of asthma, maternal smoking during pregnancy, patient history of bronchopneumonia, daycare attendance, and early exposure to pets [100].

●Most infants and young children who wheeze with RSV infection are nonatopic [96]. However, results from cohort studies have also shown that aeroallergen sensitization and wheezing with RSV and rhinovirus in early childhood are important predictors of the later development of asthma [5-9,11,79-81,101,102].

Older children — Wheezing in the setting of viral respiratory infections becomes less common and has different implications as the child becomes older. Children over two years of age with wheezing episodes after viral infection are more likely than those without virus-induced wheezing to have elevated plasma IgE levels, sensitization to inhalant allergens, and a maternal history of asthma, which suggests a predisposition to asthma [91,94,96]. Respiratory viruses other than RSV are more common triggers in older children [96]. As an example, rhinovirus was detected in 71 percent of children older than two years of age in an emergency department study of children with acute asthma exacerbations [78]. The risk of wheezing with viral infection was greatly increased with evidence of atopy (indicated by increased IgE levels) and eosinophilic inflammation.

Differential diagnosis of wheezing — The differential diagnosis of wheezing in children includes a variety of congenital and acquired conditions (table 2). Different conditions that involve a variety of anatomic airway locations can produce obstruction and expiratory or inspiratory wheezing in adults (table 3). Postnasal drip syndrome, not asthma, was the most common cause of wheeze in adult patients referred to a pulmonary outpatient clinic (figure 4). (See "Evaluation of wheezing in infants and children" and "Evaluation of wheezing illnesses other than asthma in adults".)

DEVELOPMENT OF ASTHMA — Viral respiratory tract infections in infancy, particularly with respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) and human rhinovirus (HRV), are predictive of the development of asthma in later childhood to young adulthood [5-8,11,101,103,104]. In addition, the timing of birth and the subsequent exposure to peak bronchiolitis and RSV season may influence the development of asthma [82,105]. However, it remains unclear whether certain viral respiratory infections cause asthma or if wheezing with these infections are predictors for the subsequent development of asthma [106]. (See "Risk factors for asthma", section on 'Respiratory infections'.)

Respiratory syncytial virus — In infants and children less than two years of age, respiratory infections with RSV are the most common cause of virus-induced wheezing. Many of these children have decreased wheezing with viral infections as they grow older, although RSV infections are viewed as a potential contributor to the development of asthma [6-8,107,108]. Treatment of otherwise healthy preterm infants with palivizumab to prevent severe RSV infection was associated with a decreased rate of recurrent wheeze compared with the placebo group (11 versus 21 percent, respectively) in a randomized trial of 429 high-risk infants [109]. In addition, other studies have shown that RSV lower respiratory tract infections are risk factors for the development of recurrent wheezing up to age 11 years [6-8,107,108]. The effect beyond that age is undetermined, but may be related to the severity of RSV bronchiolitis during infancy or related to other cofactors such as atopy [6-8,95,108].

Those children that have persistent symptoms of asthma or that develop asthma after RSV infection usually have other asthma risk factors, such as a maternal history of asthma and elevated immunoglobulin E (IgE) levels [77,91,97,98,110-115]. Findings from a prospective cohort study of premature infants, 146 treated with palivizumab and 171 untreated, suggest that the effect of RSV infection on subsequent recurrent wheezing is independent of atopic predisposition [116]. RSV prophylaxis decreased the risk of recurrent wheezing in children without a family history of atopy, but had no effect in children from atopic families. Precisely how RSV infections are a risk factor in the development of asthma has not been established.

Rhinovirus — Rhinovirus (the common cold virus) influences the development of asthma [5,77,79]. Studies have shown that infants who wheeze with rhinovirus infection have increased risk of recurrent wheeze and asthma at ages three, five, and six [9,80,81]. In fact, another study showed that wheezing with rhinovirus infection during the first three years of life was a much stronger risk factor for asthma at six years of age than wheezing with RSV infection or aeroallergen sensitization in a high-risk birth cohort [5].

Individual host factors, such as genetic factors and atopy, most likely interact with viral factors in susceptible patients, resulting in an increased risk of developing asthma. Specifically, studies suggest that allergic sensitization in the first year of life is the initial event that interacts with viral infections, particularly rhinovirus, to promote the development of asthma in children [79,117]. This interaction does not appear to be bidirectional, since wheezing with a viral illness did not increase the subsequent rate of allergic sensitization. However, it still remains unclear whether viral infections (HRV and RSV) play causative roles in the development of asthma or are markers of predisposed individuals. (See "Risk factors for asthma".)

Evidence suggests that genetic susceptibility may also play a role. Genotypic variants at the 17q21 locus are associated with both rhinovirus wheezing illnesses in early childhood and the development of asthma in these children [118]. Strong interactions with respect to subsequent risk of asthma were demonstrated between 17q21 genotypes and rhinovirus-induced wheezing. In addition, mutations in the gene encoding cadherin-related family member 3 (CDHR3) may be linked to increasing wheezing illnesses in children by mediating increased human rhinovirus-C (HRV-C) entry into host cells [14,119]. (See "Genetics of asthma" and "Risk factors for asthma".)

Other viruses — Some viral respiratory infections in early childhood may paradoxically decrease the development of asthma. Children with older siblings and children exposed to daycare settings may be less prone to develop asthma, possibly because they are exposed to more viral infections at a younger age [120-122]. However, these findings are difficult to interpret since these studies did not distinguish between upper and lower respiratory infections and were conducted prior to improved methods of viral detection. These are important distinctions since wheezing with lower respiratory tract infections, particularly with HRV, carries a greater risk for the subsequent development of asthma [5,76-78,123,124].

ASTHMA COMPLICATED BY VIRAL INFECTION — Viral respiratory infections are an important cause of increased pulmonary symptoms in children and adults with preexisting asthma [77,79,93,94,96,123,125]. Rhinovirus is the predominant pathogen identified in school-aged children and adult patients with acute asthma exacerbations. Infection with rhinovirus group C is associated with more severe disease in children admitted for asthma exacerbations [126-128]. The association between acetaminophen use for febrile illnesses and asthma control has also been explored.

Asthma exacerbations — Viral upper respiratory infections (URIs or colds) are the most frequent cause of asthma exacerbations. One community study, for example, was conducted to identify the role of viral infections in asthma exacerbations in 9- to 11-year-old children [73]. In this study, 108 children were followed for 13 months, during which episodes of wheezing were documented and nasal specimens obtained for culture and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) analysis for viruses. The following results were observed:

●Viruses were detected in over 80 percent of the episodes of reduced peak flow and wheezing.

●Increased symptoms of asthma usually occurred within 48 hours of developing a URI and persisted for at least two weeks.

●The most commonly identified virus was rhinovirus, accounting for approximately 60 percent of infections and wheezing episodes. Rhinovirus may contribute to seasonal asthma morbidity. One-third to one-half of weekly respiratory isolates obtained from six- to eight-year-old children with asthma contained detectable virus, with 72 to 99 percent of the viruses identified as human rhinovirus (HRV) [12].

A more subjective study of 138 adult patients with asthma found that colds were reported during 80 percent (223 of 280) of episodes of wheezing, chest tightness, or breathlessness, and 89 percent (223 of 250) of colds were associated with asthma symptoms [73].

Asthma hospitalizations — Viral infections, with HRV in particular, are the leading cause of increased rates of hospitalization for asthma that are consistently observed in late summer and early fall in many countries, including the United States, Canada, Mexico, and Israel [124,129,130]. As an example, a Canadian study documented that asthma hospitalizations among school-aged children increased predictably after return to school following summer vacation and other breaks [131]. Specifically, a "September asthma epidemic" is observed in this age group during the third week of the month, with lesser increases in asthma admissions two days later in preschool children and six days later in adults, consistent with transmission of viral infections (figure 5).

A similar peak in asthma hospitalization caused by rhinovirus infection is seen in the Southern Hemisphere in April, which also coincides with the onset of the school year [132]. A winter peak is primarily due to respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), and a spring peak is caused by both RSV and rhinovirus.

Infection susceptibility and severity — Patients with asthma do not appear to be more susceptible to rhinovirus infection than healthy controls. However, they do appear to be at greater risk for developing more severe and prolonged lower respiratory tract symptoms with infection [133].

Acetaminophen use for febrile illnesses — More frequent use of acetaminophen was associated with increased asthma-related complications in children in observational studies [134-136] and a post-hoc analysis of a randomized trial [137], which led to the recommendation by some for children with asthma to avoid acetaminophen. However, these findings were not replicated in a prospective, randomized trial comparing acetaminophen and ibuprofen use [138]. In this trial, 300 children with mild persistent asthma were randomly assigned to as-needed treatment with acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever or pain over a 48-week period. All children received standard controller therapy for asthma. There was no significant difference between the two groups in the number of asthma exacerbations requiring treatment with systemic glucocorticoids or in the number of asthma exacerbations. There was also no difference in the percent of days with good asthma control, mean inhalations per week of an albuterol rescue inhaler, and the rate of unscheduled healthcare utilization for asthma.

The association between acetaminophen use and the development of asthma is discussed in greater detail separately. (See "Risk factors for asthma", section on 'Acetaminophen'.)

SUMMARY

●Viral respiratory infections, particularly with respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) and human rhinovirus (HRV), are the most common causes of wheezing in infants and young children, and they have important influences on the development of asthma. In addition, viral infections, most often with rhinovirus, are the most common causes of wheezing or increased symptoms in patients with preexisting asthma. (See 'Introduction' above.)

●Respiratory viruses interact with host factors to promote recurrent virus-induced wheezing and the development of asthma via a number of mechanisms including increased recruitment of inflammatory cells, promotion of cytokine production, enhancement of allergic inflammation, and augmented airways hyperresponsiveness. (See 'Pathogenesis' above.)

●RSV is the most common cause of bronchiolitis and wheezing in children <2 years of age. Exposure to tobacco smoke and reduced lung function are risk factors associated with virus-induced wheezing in this age group. (See 'Wheezing due to viral infection' above.)

●Wheezing with viral respiratory infections, particularly with RSV and HRV, are important predictors of the subsequent development of asthma. However, it is unclear whether certain viral respiratory infections cause asthma or if wheezing with these infections is a predictor of childhood asthma. (See 'Development of asthma' above.)

●Viral respiratory infections are an important, and probably the most common, cause of increased pulmonary symptoms in children and adults with preexisting asthma. Rhinovirus is the predominant pathogen and the most frequent trigger of virus-induced asthma exacerbations in patients with asthma. Patients with asthma do not appear to be more susceptible to rhinovirus infection than healthy controls. However, they are at greater risk of developing more severe and prolonged lower respiratory tract symptoms with infection. (See 'Asthma complicated by viral infection' above.)

# REFERENCES

Topic 535 Version 22.0

---

# Epidemiology of asthma

Epidemiology of asthma

Authors:
    Augusto A Litonjua, MD
    Scott T Weiss, MD, MS
Section Editor:
    Peter J Barnes, DM, DSc, FRCP, FRS
Deputy Editor:
    Helen Hollingsworth, MD

Contributor Disclosures

All topics are updated as new evidence becomes available and our peer review process is complete.

Literature review current through: Feb 2018. | This topic last updated: Aug 03, 2017.

INTRODUCTION — Accurate assessment of the prevalence of asthma has been hindered by varying definitions of asthma and methods of data collection, each combining to make data comparison across studies difficult. Following a brief discussion about the definition of asthma and the identification of affected individuals, studies of asthma prevalence are reviewed. The definition, diagnosis, and treatment of asthma are discussed in detail elsewhere. (See "Diagnosis of asthma in adolescents and adults" and "An overview of asthma management".)

DATA COLLECTION — Essential to any study of prevalence are the definition of the disease being studied and the methods available to identify affected individuals. Although many definitions and methods of data collection have been used and reported, herein we discuss the most common.

Definition — A definition of asthma that incorporates both a positive test of airway hyperresponsiveness and symptoms was proposed and tested for epidemiologic purposes [1]. The study defined "current asthma" as bronchial hyperresponsiveness (BHR) in the presence of wheezing in the 12 months prior to the study. According to this definition:

●"Current asthma" identified a group of children with more severe BHR, greater peak flow variability, more symptoms, more atopy, and more medication use than children with BHR alone or wheeze alone

●Children who only had wheeze did not differ from the normal group except in reporting symptoms and in more associated use of asthma medications

●Children with BHR only were intermediate in severity, in terms of BHR and other study parameters, between the normal and current asthma groups

It appears, therefore, that this definition identifies a group of children with clinically important asthma. Because therapy can return both symptoms and BHR to the normal range, the authors recommend extending the definition of current asthma to include subjects who have had their illness recognized by a clinician. This definition may not be as useful in adults because of the effects of smoking and because BHR has been unable to adequately distinguish asthma from chronic bronchitis [2].

Questionnaires — Epidemiologists have relied primarily on historic or questionnaire sources to identify patients with asthma. Adding to the inherent limitations of questionnaires, different studies have used different symptom questionnaires, and cases have been identified either by physicians or by surveys of population groups in which the definition of who has asthma has been left to the patients themselves, the parents of patients, or to the report of the diagnosis by the patient's clinician [3]. Other studies have included objective tests of lung function and airway responsiveness in the identification of asthma.

Each method of selecting asthma patients has its inherent problems. It is likely that some bias in reporting of cases is present in each group and that the biases in each approach are different. The table summarizes the potential problems of the various approaches to identifying persons with asthma (table 1).

As part of ongoing studies, a 1996 report validated questionnaire-defined asthma and BHR versus respiratory clinician assessment of current asthma [4]. Using the Tasmanian Asthma Survey (TAS) questionnaire for adults and the International Study of Asthma and Allergies in Children (ISAAC) questionnaire for children, subjects with symptoms in the past year were identified. BHR testing was conducted using hypertonic saline. Subjects were then interviewed by one of two respiratory physicians who were blinded to the results of the questionnaires and BHR testing. A clinician diagnosis of "current asthma" was defined as a history of wheeze suggestive of a clinical diagnosis of asthma within the past 12 months.

Using the clinician's diagnosis as the "gold standard," questionnaire responses alone were found to be sensitive (0.80 for adults, 0.85 for children) and specific (0.97 for adults, 0.81 for children). Questionnaire response plus BHR was found to have high specificity (0.99 for adults, 0.94 for children) but low sensitivity (0.37 for adults, 0.47 for children). This study, however, did not incorporate a previous asthma diagnosis to the definitions of asthma, particularly for the children.

GLOBAL VARIATION — The literature is replete with studies reporting the prevalence of asthma and asthma-like symptoms from different countries around the world [5-9]. However, comparison of these prevalence estimates has been difficult given the different methods used between studies [10].

Two multicenter studies, the International Study of Asthma and Allergies in Children (ISAAC) and the European Community Respiratory Health Survey (ECRHS) are addressing this issue. Reports from ISAAC have confirmed the wide variations in the prevalence of asthma symptoms in children worldwide [5-7]. This study employed standardized simple surveys which were conducted among representative samples of school children in two age groups (6 to 7 and 13 to 14 years) in 61 countries. In a subset, a video asthma questionnaire was also administered.

For the ISAAC surveys conducted from 2000 to 2002, there was marked variation in the prevalence of wheeze in the last 12 months [7]. As an example, in the younger age group, the prevalence of wheeze ranged from 2.4 to 37.6 percent with lowest rates in India, Indonesia, the Isle of Man, and Malaysia and highest rates in Australia, Brazil, Costa Rica, New Zealand, and Panama. In the older age group, the difference in prevalence between countries ranged from 0.8 to 32.6 percent with lowest rates in China and India and the highest rates in Australia, Brazil, New Zealand, Republic of Ireland, and the United Kingdom.

For asthma prevalence, the variations were just as large as for wheeze in the last 12 months [7]. For example, among six to seven year olds, prevalence of ever having asthma ranged from 3.4 percent in Africa to 29.2 percent in Oceania; among the 13 to 14 year olds, prevalence ranged from 5.1 percent in Northern and Eastern Europe to 22 percent in Oceania. The authors point out that a striking feature of the data is the high rates of asthma symptoms (23 percent of 13 to 14 year olds) in countries whose predominant language is English.

The ECRHS is a multicenter survey of the prevalence, determinants, and management of asthma in 20 to 44 year old adults from 48 centers, predominantly in Western Europe [8]. As in the ISAAC reports, the prevalence of all symptoms varied widely. Wheeze prevalence ranged from 4.1 to 9.7 percent from centers in India, Algeria, and Italy to 23.2 to 32 percent from centers in Sweden, Denmark, Estonia, United States, United Kingdom, Australia, New Zealand, and Ireland. Asthma prevalence ranged from 2 to 3.3 percent from centers in Estonia, Germany, Spain, Greece, Austria, Algeria, and Italy, to 8 to 11.9 percent from centers in the United Kingdom, New Zealand, and Australia.

As with the ISAAC results, English speaking centers had a generally high prevalence of symptoms in the ECRHS survey. This remains unexplained and will require further study. One may speculate, however, that being in an English speaking center may be a marker for an exposure which increases the risk for asthma and asthma-like symptoms.

The Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) summarized data from both ECRHS and ISAAC in the Global Burden of Asthma report [11]. Highlights of this report are:

●As of 2004, it was estimated that as many as 300 million people of all ages suffer from asthma. By 2025, it is expected that this number will rise to 400 million worldwide.

●The rate of asthma increases as communities adopt western lifestyles and become urbanized. Despite this knowledge, the international patterns of asthma prevalence cannot be explained by the current knowledge of potential causes for the development of asthma.

●The increase in the prevalence of asthma has been associated with an increase in atopic sensitization, and is paralleled by similar increases in other allergic disorders such as eczema and rhinitis.

●It is estimated that asthma accounts for about 1 in every 250 deaths worldwide. Many of these could be prevented with optimal access to medical care.

●The direct (eg, hospital admissions and pharmaceutical costs) and indirect (eg, time lost from work or school) economic cost of asthma is considerable.

TRENDS — Data from the Centers for Disease Control have shown that the prevalence of asthma increased in the United States from the early 1980s to the mid 1990s [12,13]. During the period from 1982 to 1992, the overall annual age-adjusted prevalence rate of self-reported asthma increased by 42 percent, from 34.7 to 49.4 per 1000. For the younger age group 5 to 34 years, where the diagnosis of asthma is thought to be more accurate, the rate increased from 34.6 to 52.6 per 1000, an increase of 52 percent. Since that time, the prevalence of asthma has increased more gradually [14-16]. During the years 2001 through 2009, the age-adjusted prevalence of asthma increased from 7.3 to 8.2 percent [16]. 

Analysis of data from the 2001 to 2013 National Health Interview Survey for children aged 0 to 17 years has shown a plateau of the adjusted prevalence of asthma between 2009 and 2012, and a decline in 2013, the most recent year for which data is available [17]. Subgroup analyses showed that from 2001 to 2013, there was no change in prevalence for non-Hispanic white and Puerto Rican children and children living in the Northeastern and Western United States; continued increase in prevalence among 10 to 17 year olds, poor children, and those living in the Southern United States; increase followed by a plateau among 5 to 9 year olds, near-poor children, and non-Hispanic black children; and increase followed by a decrease in prevalence among 0 to 4 year olds, non-poor, Mexican children, and those living in the Midwestern United States. Puerto Rican children remained the subgroup with the highest asthma prevalence as of 2013 [17].

As noted above, asthma prevalence varies around the world. While most countries saw an increase in prevalence of asthma toward the end of the last century, data since 2000 has shown differing trends. While asthma prevalence continues to rise after the year 2000 in some countries such as Italy [18] and Sweden [19], other countries such as Denmark [20] and Korea [21] have seen a plateau of prevalence from data through 2010 and 2014, respectively.

The annual death rate from asthma in the United States also increased from 1982 to 1995, but has declined since then (figure 1) [15]. Consistently higher rates have been reported for blacks than for whites, but not for Hispanic or Latino ethnicity [15]. This difference in death rates is thought to be due both to socioeconomic factors and to differences in access to medical care [14,22].

These overall trends are not unique to the United States. Use of the same methods in other countries has also shown that the prevalence of asthma is on the rise [23].

Some areas have seen a moderation or even a decline in mortality. For example, in an analysis of data between 1969 and 1993 in New Zealand, mortality rates peaked in 1979 and began falling in the early 1980s to a low of 0.5 deaths per 100,000 in 1993 [24]. According to an analysis of asthma mortality data from 20 countries, asthma death rates, which increased during the 1980s, have significantly decreased subsequently to a level of 0.23 deaths per 100,000 in 2004 to 2005 [25-27].

Several explanations for the observed increase in prevalence have been suggested. Increased clinician and public awareness of the signs and symptoms of asthma could have led to a change in diagnostic recognition and accuracy. However, changes in disease classification and the effects of the 1979 ICD revision have probably had only a minor impact. It is likely that changes in the risk factors thought to cause and worsen asthma are responsible for much of the increase in asthma prevalence [28]. 

Similarly, the reasons for the plateau and potential decrease in prevalence of asthma in some countries remain unclear. It has been hypothesized that because of the rapidly changing exposures and lifestyles in the latter half of the past century, the proportion of the population that was susceptible to developing asthma was exposed (and is still being exposed, in some countries), and is now reaching capacity [29]. It is also possible that better understanding of risk factors and faster dissemination of this information to the public may have led to these changes in the trends. 

SOCIETY GUIDELINE LINKS — Links to society and government-sponsored guidelines from selected countries and regions around the world are provided separately. (See "Society guideline links: Asthma in adults".)

INFORMATION FOR PATIENTS — UpToDate offers two types of patient education materials, “The Basics” and “Beyond the Basics.” The Basics patient education pieces are written in plain language, at the 5th to 6th grade reading level, and they answer the four or five key questions a patient might have about a given condition. These articles are best for patients who want a general overview and who prefer short, easy-to-read materials. Beyond the Basics patient education pieces are longer, more sophisticated, and more detailed. These articles are written at the 10th to 12th grade reading level and are best for patients who want in-depth information and are comfortable with some medical jargon.

Here are the patient education articles that are relevant to this topic. We encourage you to print or e-mail these topics to your patients. (You can also locate patient education articles on a variety of subjects by searching on “patient info” and the keyword(s) of interest.)

●Beyond the Basics topics (see "Patient education: Asthma treatment in adolescents and adults (Beyond the Basics)" and "Patient education: Trigger avoidance in asthma (Beyond the Basics)")

SUMMARY

●Accurate assessment of the prevalence of asthma has been hindered by varying definitions of asthma and methods of data collection. A definition of "current asthma" as bronchial hyperresponsiveness (BHR) in the presence of wheezing in the preceding 12 months identifies a group of children with clinically important asthma, ie, more severe BHR, greater peak flow variability, more symptoms, more atopy, and more medication use than children with BHR alone or wheeze alone. However, this definition may not be as useful in adults because of the effects of smoking and because BHR has been unable to adequately distinguish asthma from chronic bronchitis. (See 'Definition' above.)

●Epidemiologists have relied primarily on historic or questionnaire sources to identify patients with asthma. However, it is likely that some bias in reporting of cases is associated with each of the various approaches and that the biases differ among the approaches. The table summarizes the potential problems in identifying persons with asthma (table 1). (See 'Questionnaires' above.)

●Many studies have evaluated the prevalence of asthma and asthma like symptoms in different countries around the world, although comparison of these prevalence estimates is difficult given the different methods used. (See 'Global variation' above.)

●Two multinational studies, the International Study of Asthma and Allergies in Children (ISAAC) and the European Community Respiratory Health Survey (ECRHS) are monitoring the prevalence of asthma and asthma like symptoms. Reports from ISAAC have confirmed wide variations in the prevalence of asthma symptoms in children worldwide. (See 'Global variation' above.)

●Data from the Centers for Disease Control have shown that the prevalence of asthma increased in the United States from the early 1980s to the mid 1990s. During the years 2001 through 2009, the prevalence of asthma increased more gradually, and then remained stable in the years 2009 to 2012 with a decline in 2013. (See 'Trends' above.)
